cataloging_subject.label: The Chronicle of England, Part 1
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual work is Robert Mannyng of Brunne's first chronicle part, ending at Furnivall's printed line 16,688. It is one Middle English verse work, not the whole two-part chronicle, not Furnivall's two-volume 1887 publication, and not the complete U-M/CME encoded item. The separately modeled representation context includes the forty-two-line transition into Part II (printed lines 16,689-16,730), Furnivall's NOTE1 apparatus, and zero-count front and back matter.

catalog_summary.title: The Chronicle of England, Part 1
catalog_summary.author: Robert Mannyng of Brunne
catalog_summary.date: Completed in 1338; first-part boundary ends at Furnivall line 16,688
catalog_summary.region: England, with Sixhills in Lincolnshire as a supported authorial and compositional context rather than an unqualified exact place of composition

identifiers.scheme: Library of Congress call number in U-M header

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus cataloger, following MEC/MED BIB2350's Part 1 title and Furnivall's explicit boundary

assertion.notes: This supplied title distinguishes the bounded first part from titles used for the complete two-part chronicle and for Furnivall's publication.

scope.description: Furnivall and U-M title applied to the larger two-part publication and reused here only as a source title for the bounded first part

notes: The source title is not used as proof that the two parts constitute one cataloging unit.

scope.description: DIMEV title for the aggregate two-part verse chronicle

agents.name: Robert Mannyng of Brunne
notes: The U-M source header gives Mannyng, Robert, fl. 1288-1338; DIMEV gives Robert Brunne.

assertion.notes: Translation chiefly describes Mannyng's use of Wace; it does not imply line-for-line equivalence.
This is responsibility for the 1887 source edition, not medieval authorship.
The date belongs to the medieval chronicle, not to the surviving manuscript copies, the 1887 edition, or the 2006 encoding.
Sixhills is retained as supported authorial and compositional context; the draft does not convert that context into an exact place-of-composition assertion.

language_statements.label: Middle English

assertion.notes: The U-M header's generic eng is normalized to enm for discovery.

notes: Brief embedded Latin and modern English editorial material do not make the medieval work bilingual or mixed-language for discovery.

form_statements.description: A verse chronicle in rhyming couplets. A brief direct BODY prose paragraph containing a Latin antiphon, editorial NOTE1 prose, rubrics, and display conventions do not make the one count-bearing medieval work a mixed-form item.

subject_statements.label: Great Britain -- History
subject_statements.scheme: U-M source subject normalized as a General Corpus tag

summaries.value: Robert Mannyng's first chronicle part recounts legendary and early British history in Middle English rhyming couplets, chiefly adapting Wace's Roman de Brut while drawing selectively on other historical authorities. The bounded work ends with the six-line Latin epitaph through Furnivall line 16,688; the ensuing forty-two lines announce and begin Part II and are not counted as part of this work.

The intellectual target is printed lines 1-16,688 on pp. 1-579. Printed lines 16,689-16,730 on pp. 579-580 are a transition into Part II. Furnivall's NOTE1 apparatus contributes 345 encoded L elements in four LG elements but no additional work item; front and back matter likewise contribute no work items.

extent.description: Raw representation accounting for the complete encoded item: 281 top-level DIV1 containers (FRONT 2, BODY 278, BACK 1), 436 LG and 16,974 L. The cataloged intellectual target is one verse work through printed line 16,688 and accounts for 431 direct BODY LG and 16,587 direct BODY L. Direct BODY representation totals 432 LG and 16,629 L after adding the one-LG, forty-two-line Part II transition. Furnivall's NOTE1 apparatus adds four LG and 345 L, producing the raw 436/16,974 totals; front and back matter add no LG or L. Furnivall's terminal printed label is 16,730, which implies 16,630 numbered positions because labels jump from 12,899 to 13,000. The XML nevertheless has 16,629 direct BODY L because printed p. 360 labels 10,296-10,309 span thirteen encoded rows rather than fourteen; this is a numbering anomaly, not a missing encoded row. Item and form counts describe the one intellectual target, while section/LG/L totals intentionally describe the containing representation.

content_parts.label: Encoded editorial front matter
content_parts.summary: Two FRONT/DIV1 containers preceding the medieval target; retained for representation accounting but not counted as an intellectual work item.

content_parts.label: The Chronicle of England, Part 1
content_parts.summary: The sole count-bearing intellectual target, from the opening through the Latin epitaph ending at Furnivall's printed line 16,688.

locators.value: BODY opening through printed line 16,688; 431 direct BODY LG and 16,587 direct BODY L; U-M image sequences 00000035-00000613
PDF pp. 13-137, ending within PDF p. 137
target principally fols. 1ra-95va; Part II begins on fol. 95b
target begins at surviving fol. 1r after loss of its first leaf and runs broadly through fol. 71v; exact terminal column remains unresolved
fol. 4r-v; fragment corresponding to printed lines 13,018-13,193

notes: This is the only part with item_count 1. All other modeled parts are zero-count representation or scope controls.

content_parts.label: Embedded Latin passages
content_parts.summary: Short Latin rubrics and quotations within the Middle English verse work, including a direct BODY prose antiphon and the six-line terminal epitaph; the parent owns their representation counts.

locators.value: direct BODY P after line 15,092 on printed p. 524; terminal six-line epitaph at printed lines 16,683-16,688; additional short rubrics and headings

notes: Metrics are zero to prevent double counting: these passages occur inside the parent work's 431 LG / 16,587 L representation.

content_parts.label: Transition into Part II
content_parts.summary: Forty-two direct BODY verse lines after the target boundary, announcing and beginning Part II; represented but not counted as part of ahb1379.

locators.value: lines 16,689-16,730; remainder of printed p. 579 and all of p. 580; U-M sequences 00000613-00000614
remainder of PDF p. 137 and PDF p. 138

notes: The rubric Incipiunt Gesta Anglorum and the Petyt marker 95b:2 confirm the transition into the next part.

content_parts.label: Furnivall NOTE1 apparatus
content_parts.summary: Editorial notes nested throughout the encoding. Their quoted verse is counted for raw representation reconciliation but not as an additional intellectual work item.

locators.value: all NOTE1 descendants; 6,128 NOTE1 elements, including four LG and 345 L

notes: Petyt material quoted in apparatus after the main-text supply ending at line 4,918 continues through line 4,942 but is excluded from the main-text transcription stream.

content_parts.label: Encoded editorial back matter
content_parts.summary: One BACK/DIV1 container following the BODY; retained for representation accounting but not counted as an intellectual work item.

related_works.title: Roman de Brut
notes: Wace is the principal source for the bounded first part; Mannyng also translates and reshapes the material.

related_works.title: Historia ecclesiastica gentis Anglorum
notes: A limited historical source, not the principal base text.

related_works.title: Historia regum Britanniae
notes: Relevant through the chronicle tradition and Furnivall's source discussion; the draft does not flatten Wace and Geoffrey into equivalent direct bases.

related_works.title: Chronicle of Peter of Langtoft
notes: Langtoft is important to the larger two-part project, especially Part II; that role is not allowed to erase the Part I boundary.

evidence.citation: Pinned CME XML for U-M item AHB1379.0001.001
evidence.locator: HEADER; FRONT, BODY, and BACK; all DIV1, LG, L, NOTE1, P, PB, and MILESTONE elements; XPath and manual text inspection on 2026-07-16
evidence.summary: Supports identifiers, source edition, encoding history, subject, exact work/transition boundary, element counts, line-number anomalies, scoped Latin, Petyt markers, and the separation of target text from apparatus and surrounding matter.

evidence.citation: Robert Mannyng of Brunne, The Story of England, ed. Frederick James Furnivall, vol. I, Rolls Series 87 (London, 1887)
evidence.locator: title page, introduction, editorial source statements, and target text pp. 1-454 / PDF pp. 38-491
evidence.summary: Supports the 1887 first-volume expression, title, editor, composition date and context, principal Wace source, witness use, and the first portion of the target through printed line 13,108.

evidence.citation: Robert Mannyng of Brunne, The Story of England, ed. Frederick James Furnivall, vol. II, Rolls Series 87 (London, 1887)
evidence.locator: title page and target text pp. 455-579 / PDF pp. 13-137; Part II transition lines 16,689-16,730 on pp. 579-580 / PDF pp. 137-138
evidence.summary: Supports the second-volume expression, the exact target ending at line 16,688, the following transition, embedded Latin passages, Petyt and Lambeth source markers, and terminal line-number accounting.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 3253
evidence.locator: record heading and five witness entries
evidence.summary: Identifies Robert Brunne, the Metrical Chronicle title, chronicle subject, couplet form, and five reported witness records. The lineage manifest narrows those aggregate entries to three witnesses securely overlapping the bounded Part I target.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, A Descriptive Catalogue of the Manuscripts in the Library of Lambeth Palace: The Mediaeval Manuscripts (Cambridge, 1932)
evidence.locator: entry for Lambeth Palace Library MS 131
evidence.summary: Supports the fifteenth-century date, paper support, 134-leaf extent, two-hand description, lost first leaf, and broad target placement in Lambeth MS 131.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for ahb1379
evidence.summary: Supplies the reviewed distinction among manuscript witnesses, edition volumes, scan carriers, U-M page images, repository artifact, access conditions, and rights assertions.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium / Middle English Dictionary bibliography record BIB2350
evidence.locator: record title, 1338 date, and Part I witness references
evidence.summary: The exact MEC/MED bibliography record used in pre-draft research supports the normalized Part 1 title, 1338 date, and the Petyt 511.7, Lambeth 131, and Rawlinson D.913 fol. 4 target census when read with Furnivall and DIMEV. Current automated retrieval returned a provider 403 challenge, so no local HTML copy or search-result snippet is represented as direct cached evidence.
notes: The 403/cache-negative condition is preserved explicitly. Assertions also cite directly cached Furnivall, DIMEV, XML, James, and MARCO evidence rather than relying on the inaccessible record alone.

open_questions.question: Can the exact place of composition be established more narrowly than the supported Sixhills/Lincolnshire authorial context?
next_steps: Review current Mannyng scholarship before changing the associated-place statement into a place-of-composition assertion.

open_questions.question: Does Gloucester Cathedral Library MS 36, fragment 8 overlap the bounded Part I target, and if so at what exact lines?
next_steps: Obtain a direct catalog description or images of fragment 8 before treating it as a Part I source witness.

notes: The one-item boundary ends at printed line 16,688. The following forty-two direct BODY lines are explicitly represented but never counted as part of ahb1379.
The extent deliberately reports both intellectual counts and raw encoding counts. Raw LG/L totals are not treated as proof of additional works.

Only Inner Temple Petyt 511.7, Lambeth Palace Library MS 131, and Bodleian Rawlinson D.913 fol. 4 are bound as secure witnesses to the bounded Part I target. Merton 23.b.6 is explicitly Part II, and Gloucester fragment 8 remains unscoped.
The discovery language is enm only. Scoped Latin and modern editorial English are preserved in language statements without labeling the work bilingual or mixed-language.
